robotics_lab.name: roomba
robotics_lab.description:
The Roomba is an autonomous robotic vacuum cleaner developed by iRobot and introduced in 2002. It features a range of sensors that help it navigate and clean floor areas by detecting obstacles and avoiding drops, such as stairs. Over time, Roomba models have evolved with advanced technologies, including more powerful vacuums, tangle-free brushes, and improved obstacle avoidance capabilities. Notably, newer models integrate a camera along with onboard mapping software, enabling Roombas to systematically cover rooms, avoid obstacles like pet waste and cords, and find charging stations. Roombas are also equipped with acoustic and optical sensors for dirt detection, allowing them to target particularly dirty spots on floors. As of 2024, the product lineup spans from the fourth to the tenth generation, each offering different features, from basic cleaning to performance mapping via mobile apps and voice assistant integration.
            The development of the Roomba began in 1989 when engineer Joe Jones conceived the idea at the MIT Computer Science and AI Laboratory. After initial setbacks and prototype presentations to companies like Denning Mobile Robot and Bissell, S.C. Johnson funded the project in 1999, which eventually led to the creation of the Roomba in 2002. Roomba's design is optimized for fitting under furniture, with most models measuring around 33-35 cm in diameter. The vacuum includes various sensors, including infrared cliff sensors to avoid drops, contact-sensing bumpers, and dirt detection sensors. Advanced models like the 500, 700, and 800 series feature Virtual Wall and Virtual Wall Lighthouse accessories to manage cleaning zones, and some later models are capable of self-adjusting brush directions to escape entangled cords. The Roomba's intuitive design and integration with mobile apps allow users to monitor, control, and schedule cleaning tasks remotely.

robotics_lab.name: Pepper
robotics_lab.description:
Pepper is a semi-humanoid robot developed by Aldebaran Robotics (formerly SoftBank Robotics Europe), designed to read and respond to human emotions. Introduced in Japan in June 2014, it uses facial recognition and voice tone analysis to detect emotions, and it was marketed for use in retail, hospitality, and healthcare environments. Despite an initial surge in popularity, with the first batch of 1,000 units selling out in 60 seconds, production was paused in June 2021 due to weak demand. As of 2025, Aldebaran Robotics went into receivership, signaling the end of the robot's future. The robot's applications ranged from reception duties in offices to greeting passengers at airports and assisting in medical and banking facilities. It was also deployed in restaurants and cafes in Japan, and its voice library was later made available to the public in 2024. At its peak, around 27,000 units were sold globally, including deployments in Europe, North America, and the UAE.
            Pepper is designed not for household use but to enhance human interaction and entertainment. Its specifications include a range of sensors: microphones, HD cameras, 3D depth sensors, and touch sensors in the head and hands, as well as sonar, lasers, and gyroscopes in its mobile base for navigation and interaction. With its expressive capabilities, Pepper has also been used in academic settings for research into human-robot interactions and elder care, notably through the CARESSES project, which aimed to develop a culturally competent robot for elderly care. While not intended to replace humans, Pepper was designed to assist and enrich lives through interaction, making it a popular tool in social and educational contexts. The robot's design encourages independent developers to create new uses, keeping it versatile and adaptable to various industries and research areas.

robotics_lab.name: Lynxmotion
robotics_lab.description:
The Lynxmotion Robotic Arm is a versatile, multi-degree-of-freedom robotic arm designed for a wide range of applications, from educational use to hobbyist projects and industrial automation prototypes. Typically powered by servo motors, this robotic arm can perform complex tasks like pick-and-place operations, assembly work, and even simple interactions with objects. The arm is highly customizable, with users able to adjust the length of its links, change the types of grippers, and modify its range of motion. It is often controlled using a microcontroller like Arduino, and it integrates easily with various sensors, making it an ideal platform for learning about kinematics, programming, and robotics control systems.
            A standout feature of the Lynxmotion Robotic Arm is its modular design, which allows for easy upgrades and customization. The arm's components are designed for quick assembly, making it beginner-friendly while still offering depth for more experienced users who want to dive into advanced robotics. It can be controlled through multiple interfaces, including direct servo control, serial communication, or more advanced setups like inverse kinematics for precision movement. The arm is often used in classrooms and workshops, but it's also popular among hobbyists looking to build a functional robot that can be expanded with sensors, vision systems, and other advanced technologies.

robotics_lab.name: Culturally Sensitive Social Robotics for Africa
robotics_lab.description: The CSSR4Africa (Culturally Sensitive Social Robotics for Africa) project aims to address the cultural acceptability of social robots in African societies, recognizing that successful robot deployment hinges on these machines being accepted by local populations. Specifically, the project focuses on adapting robot behavior to reflect culturally sensitive patterns of interaction that are predictable and socially acceptable to African citizens. Rather than developing these behaviors through extensive trial-and-error interaction, the project leverages ethnographic research to identify culturally appropriate interaction patterns across various African cultures. The goal is to encapsulate these insights into reconfigurable interaction primitives—standardized, reusable behavioral templates that can be applied across different social contexts and robot applications. These primitives will help design social robots that exhibit respectful, courteous, and engaging behavior, aligning with local social and cultural norms rather than imposing foreign or inappropriate modes of interaction from Western or Eastern cultures.
